title: Writing - Part 2
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 140-190 words.

tasks.title: Email
tasks.prompt:
Your English friend Sam is visiting your country for the first time and has asked you to recommend some cultural experiences and traditions they should try. Write an email to Sam describing two or three cultural traditions or experiences and explaining why they would enjoy them.

Write your email to Sam.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Hi Sam,

I am so excited for your visit — you are going to love it here! There are so many things I want to show you, but let me tell you about a few cultural experiences that I think will really stay with you.

First, you absolutely have to come to a local market day. Ours happens every Saturday morning, and it is a wonderful mix of food, crafts, music, and people. The smell of freshly baked bread and grilled food is irresistible. It is also the best place to try local specialities and chat with artisan producers.

Another tradition I would love to share with you is our Midsummer festival. It is a huge outdoor celebration with traditional music, dancing, and lanterns, and the atmosphere is magical — especially in the evening when everyone gathers around bonfires.

Finally, I will take you to meet my grandmother, who makes traditional embroidery. Watching her work is a genuine window into the past.

Can't wait!

All the best,
Katya

tasks.title: Article
tasks.prompt:
You see this notice in an English-language magazine:

'Cultural traditions are disappearing as the world becomes more globalised. Write an article discussing whether it is important to preserve local customs and traditions, and what we can do to keep them alive.'

Write your article.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Roots and Routes: Why Cultural Traditions Still Matter

In a world where the same coffee chains, fashion brands, and music artists appear in cities from Tokyo to Buenos Aires, it is easy to feel that local cultures are slowly being swallowed by a bland global sameness. Should we be worried? I believe we should.

Cultural traditions — whether they are festivals, food, music, language, or craft — are not merely picturesque relics. They are the accumulated wisdom and identity of communities, connecting generations and giving people a sense of belonging that nothing else quite replicates. When a traditional language dies or a centuries-old festival is abandoned, something irreplaceable is lost.

However, preservation does not mean freezing culture in amber. Traditions evolve naturally, and that is healthy. The key is to ensure that communities themselves drive that evolution, rather than having their cultures replaced wholesale by external commercial forces.

Schools, local governments, and cultural institutions all have a role to play. But ultimately, it begins with each of us choosing to value what makes our corner of the world unique.

Diversity is not a problem to be solved. It is the world's greatest treasure.

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt:
You read a news article about a traditional festival in your area being cancelled due to lack of funding. Write a letter to the local council arguing that the festival should be saved, explaining its cultural importance and suggesting ways to fund it.

Write your letter.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Members of the Council,

I am writing in response to the announcement that the annual Harvest Festival — a tradition that has brought our community together for over a hundred years — is to be cancelled due to insufficient funding. I urge you most strongly to reconsider this decision.

The Harvest Festival is not simply a pleasant event. It is a living piece of our town's cultural heritage and an occasion that draws visitors from across the region, generating income for local businesses. For many residents, particularly older members of the community, it represents a deeply meaningful annual tradition with personal and family associations that span generations.

Regarding funding, I would suggest several possible solutions. Local businesses might be approached as commercial sponsors in exchange for prominent branding at the event. A crowdfunding campaign could also prove effective — community goodwill towards the festival is enormous. Additionally, a small entrance fee for certain activities, while keeping general admission free, could raise sufficient funds without deterring visitors.

Please do not allow this beloved tradition to disappear without exploring every possible option.

Yours faithfully,
Robert Ashford
